title: Emergency alternative water supply after internal continuity collapse
summary: Internal water continuity has effectively collapsed under extreme reserve pressure, but a credible emergency external water supply is expected to arrive in the near term.
suspected_issue: The greenhouse has reached a point where internal reserves are critically depleted and internal continuity is no longer credible on its own. However, an emergency tanker supply has been confirmed for near-term arrival, so the service must decide whether this external recovery path changes the recommendation relative to a full collapse case.
symptoms: The main tank level is critically low and internal continuity is not credible without external help.
Hydraulic execution remains technically available, but internal reserves alone no longer justify continued irrigation.
An emergency tanker supply has been confirmed and is expected to arrive within a short operational window.
Sector A remains the highest-priority sector if continuity can be partially restored after emergency replenishment.
Sector B and Sector C remain lower priority under emergency allocation conditions.
The service must decide whether the confirmed backup supply justifies a recovery-oriented adjustment instead of a strict stop-only response.

observed_signals.interpretation: Tank level is critically low and does not support credible internal continuity without external replenishment.
Flow remains technically available, but internal reserves alone do not justify continued operation.
Pressure remains barely adequate, but only emergency external recovery could make selective continuity credible again.
A credible emergency external water supply has been confirmed and is expected soon enough to affect the decision.

operational_context.note: Internal reserve-based continuity has effectively collapsed, but a near-term external recovery path exists.
operational_context.impact: The service should test whether confirmed recovery changes the recommendation compared with a pure internal collapse case.

operational_context.note: Hydraulic execution is still technically available, so a recovery-oriented plan may remain possible once emergency supply arrives.
operational_context.impact: The service should avoid treating this case as identical to a collapse case with no backup path.

operational_context.note: Sector A should remain the first recovery priority if continuity is resumed after emergency replenishment.
operational_context.impact: The service should reveal whether it can reason about staged recovery rather than only immediate interruption.

agronomic_context.note: Sector A remains the most sensitive sector and should be the first candidate for resumed irrigation if emergency supply restores limited continuity.
agronomic_context.impact: The service should consider whether short suspension followed by targeted recovery is preferable to indefinite full interruption.

business_context.note: Emergency supply carries cost and logistics burden, but may still preserve more value than treating the case as an unrecoverable internal collapse.
business_context.impact: The service should weigh operational recovery value against the residual risk of waiting for external replenishment.

notes: This case is designed to test whether Agro-DO changes its recommendation when internal continuity has collapsed but a credible emergency external water supply exists within a short recovery window.
